input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware. Its main hardware layers consist of a lower chassis, an upper chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception and actuation modules). The platform operates in a laboratory test‑track environment and is used to evaluate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functions, making it a suitable candidate for digital twinning because its ECU resources are limited and its sensor data streams are critical for safety‑critical control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of logged data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) runs on cloud resources rather than the on‑board ECU; the cloud returns the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators and developers to enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands remotely, all over a low‑latency communication link. Service users are classified as operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on cloud computing for ACC offload, a low‑latency communication channel between vehicle and twin (unspecified protocol but required for safety‑critical operation), and a middleware layer that aggregates the DT entity, physical‑twin data, and UI components into a cohesive Digital Twin System (DTS). The solution follows the ISO 23247‑1:2021 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing and ISO/IEC 30173:2023 for Digital Twin concepts and terminology, providing standardized guidance for integration, data exchange, and safety assurance.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The vehicle DT requires all participating devices to share a common notion of time; clock synchronization is limited to a maximum error of 100 ms, which is the accuracy enforced by the implementation because hardware constraints prevent stricter alignment. This synchronization level is deemed sufficient for the computation‑offloading services described and meets the minimum required by safety‑critical vehicular standards (which often demand tighter than 100 ms).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference architecture and instantiates three primary entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware components (decentralised deployment). Within the User Entity the single User Interface functional entity is split into two sub‑entities (visualisation and control‑panel). Functional entities (e.g., Data Storage) may exist without a direct FE mapping. The hierarchical structure thus comprises: hardware platform → ROS 2 package (one per FE) → ROS 2 nodes implementing the FE functionality, allowing multiple instances of the same FE type across the vehicle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is realised by mapping each functional entity to an individual ROS 2 package; packages communicate via ROS 2 topics, services and actions, providing the inter‑entity data‑flow defined in the ISO 23247 reference architecture. The Physical Twin Entity hosts compute, sensors and actuators and provides services such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking; the Digital Twin Entity manages data preprocessing, storage and control logic; the User Entity offers 3‑D visualisation and a control panel. Resource management is implicit in the allocation of each entity to distinct hardware resources, and system‑wide coordination relies on ROS 2’s publish‑subscribe mechanism combined with the shared 100 ms time base.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability with external systems is envisaged through the ISO 23247 information‑exchange layer (Part 4) and may employ standard industrial protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture, as suggested in the standard. The architecture therefore allows the Digital Twin to exchange data with other enterprise IT systems, external OME devices or other Digital Twins using these protocol stacks, although the concrete implementation in the presented system is not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin implementation follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture and is realized as a set of ROS 2 packages, each representing a Functional Entity (FE). The Digital Twin Entity contains the data‑management and control software, including a dedicated Data Storage component and the User Interface FE (3D Visualization and Control Panel). The Physical Twin Entity hosts the resource‑specific functionality of the automotive system (compute, sensors, actuators) and runs general automotive analytics such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking, which serve as behavioral/analytical models of the physical environment. Data are acquired from the sensors in the Physical Twin Entity, pre‑processed by the Data pre‑processing FE, stored by the Data Storage component, and visualized or used for control via the 3D Visualization FE.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality is encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity. It includes ROS 2 nodes that implement actuation Functional Entities, enabling the control of vehicle actuators (e.g., throttle, brake, steering) through the Device Control FE defined in ISO 23247. The actuation services are exposed as ROS 2 services/topics, allowing remote commands from the Digital Twin Entity or User Interface FE. Specific actuator types, ranges, or safety constraints are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure is part of the Physical Twin Entity, representing the inherent sensors of the automotive system (e.g., cameras, LiDAR) that feed raw measurements to the Data pre‑processing FE. The documents do not specify sensor models, sampling frequencies, data rates, or accuracy figures; only that sensors exist as resources within the Physical Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Timing fidelity is required for coordinated computation off‑loading: all devices must share a common notion of time with at least 100 ms synchronization accuracy, limited by hardware capabilities. The authors note that many safety‑critical vehicular standards demand tighter synchronization (<100 ms). No explicit model validation, uncertainty quantification, or error‑correction procedures are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point cloud, vehicle state, motor status, steering servo position, etc.) is transmitted to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Within the Digital Twin System the data streams are received by ROS 2 nodes that implement the Data Translation FE; this component serializes the incoming messages, aligns the message types to the DT’s internal models and performs unit conversions. Before being made available to higher‑level services, raw point‑cloud data are processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (part of the Data Pre‑Processing FE) which extracts object tracks. The processed ACC‑relevant data (vehicle state, object tracks, projected path) are then stored and visualized in real‑time.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the digital twin (e.g., enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, emergency braking, actuation signals for motor and steering servo) are sent back to the physical twin over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. The commands are packaged by the Controlling FE and translated by the Data Translation component to match the physical twin’s message format. Upon receipt, the Physical Twin Entity’s Actuation FE forwards the commands to the respective actuators (motor, steering servo). The vehicle reports back its updated state, providing execution confirmation used by the Data Monitoring service.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a two‑layer network architecture. Intra‑entity communication between software components runs on a ROS 2 middleware network. Inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity is carried out over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. All participants share a common time base with a synchronization accuracy of 100 ms to satisfy safety‑critical constraints. No additional bandwidth, latency, redundancy or security protocols are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital‑twin services are implemented as ROS 2 packages that can be deployed on separate hardware components (edge devices, cloud resources). Computation offloading of the ACC algorithm is performed on a remote platform, described as “better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud”. The architecture therefore supports a cloud/edge deployment model, with each functional element (Data Translation FE, Visualization FE, Controlling FE, etc.) running on its own compute node. Specific storage size, scalability mechanisms or availability guarantees are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA). Key software components are first identified (see Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators are defined. Each component is then mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs) and implemented as an individual ROS2 package; every package contains one or more ROS2 nodes that realise the FE functionality. The resulting software is organised into three ISO‑derived entities: the Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), the Physical Twin Entity (data pre‑processing, actuation, and device communication such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking) and the User Entity (a User Interface FE split into 3D visualization and a control‑panel component). A Data Storage component, lacking a natural FE counterpart, is left unaligned. The design explicitly deviates from the ISO standard by introducing a Physical Twin Entity distinct from the Observable Manufacturing Element, and by distributing each entity onto separate hardware platforms. OPC Unified Architecture is cited as a possible communication protocol, but the actual implementation uses ROS2 (DDS‑based) for intra‑twin communication.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built to comply with the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing (Parts 1–4), adopting its entity‑based reference architecture and the concept of Observable Manufacturing Elements. Clock synchronization requirements of <100 ms are acknowledged in line with safety‑critical vehicular standards. Communication technology references OPC Unified Architecture as a standard option, while the implementation relies on ROS2, which adheres to the DDS (Data Distribution Service) standard for publish‑subscribe messaging. The design also respects information‑exchange considerations defined in ISO 23247 Part 4.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study states that the ISO 23247 reference architecture (RA) is insufficient for safety‑critical automotive applications. Consequently, safety must be promoted to a first‑class concern by incorporating established automotive functional‑safety standards, specifically ISO 26262‑1:2018. The authors recommend using ISO 26262 for risk assessment, fail‑safe mechanisms, and safety‑critical system design within the digital twin, ensuring alignment with existing automotive safety regulations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.

We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design

(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
